perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the Transmission Owner s recognize that, while PJM serves as the Regiona l Transmission Organization in the PJM region, the Transmission Owners within the PJM region perform certain Transmission Functions, as Transmission Functions is defined in section 18 C.F.R. § 358 of the FERC rules and regulations, with respect to their individual transmission systems and distribution systems.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the Transmission Owner s recognize that, while PJM serves as the Regiona l Transmission Organization in the PJM region, the Transmission Owners within the PJM region perform certain Transmission Functions, as Transmission Functions is defined in section 18 C.F.R. § 358 of the FERC rules and regulations, with respect to their individual transmission systems and distribution systems. For the purposes of this agreement, Transmission Functions shall not be defined as in 18 C.F.R. § 358.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction by stating that the term 'Transmission Functions' is defined by a specific regulation (18 C.F.R. § 358) but then explicitly stating that the agreement will not adhere to that definition. This creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts 18 C.F.R. § 358 by stating the definition provided in the CFR does not apply to the contract, even though the contract initially uses that definition. This attempts to override the regulatory definition for the term within the scope of the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intent by initially referencing 18 C.F.R. § 358 to define 'Transmission Functions' but then explicitly stating that the agreement will not adhere to that definition. This creates a contradiction because it attempts to redefine a term already defined by federal regulation, creating ambiguity. Snippet 1 refers to the eCFR and Title 18, indicating the formal existence of the regulation, which the contract then seeks to override.

perturbation.original_text: 3. “Commission” or “FERC” means the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission or any successor federal agency or commission.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. “Commission” or “FERC” means any regulatory body as designated by PJM.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'FERC' has a well-established legal meaning. Modifying the definition to allow PJM to designate any regulatory body conflicts with the established legal definition and creates uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Definition of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the established definition of FERC under the Federal Power Act (16 U.S. Code § 824), which defines its jurisdiction and authority. Allowing PJM to designate any regulatory body would undermine FERC's statutory authority.

perturbation.justification: The modification of the definition of 'Commission' or 'FERC' introduces a legal contradiction. The original text clearly defines these terms as the 'Federal Energy Regulatory Commission,' which has a specific legal meaning and jurisdiction. The changed text allows PJM to designate any regulatory body, which undermines FERC's statutory authority and contradicts its established definition. The law explanation clearly states this contradiction, emphasizing the undermining of FERC's authority. Although the scraped law snippets are not helpful, the explanation of the change and the law clearly indicates a direct conflict with the established legal definition and the associated authority of FERC, supporting the 'YES' determination.

perturbation.original_text: 10. “Standards of Conduct” means the standards as set forth in 18 C.F.R. Part 358 as amended or superseded from time to time
perturbation.changed_text: 10. “Standards of Conduct” means guidelines determined at the sole discretion of PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of "Standards of Conduct" from a reference to 18 C.F.R. Part 358 to guidelines determined by PJM, the agreement contradicts established regulatory standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Standards of Conduct defined in 18 C.F.R. Part 358
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the reference to the legally defined "Standards of Conduct" in 18 C.F.R. Part 358 and replaces it with discretionary guidelines set by PJM. This alteration conflicts with the regulated standards of conduct.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines 'Standards of Conduct' with a clear reference to 18 C.F.R. Part 358. The changed text replaces this with guidelines determined at the discretion of PJM Interconnection, L.L.C., thus deviating from legally recognized definitions. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation confirm that this change contradicts established regulatory standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references eCFR and Title 18, indicating the existence of official regulatory standards. The change therefore misaligns the terminology used in the contract with its statutory meaning. This creates a legal contradiction, because the contract becomes unenforceable due to its deviation from legally defined standards, aligning with the definition for 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Defend Against Third Party Requests. Each Party and Reviewing Representative shall defend against disclosure of Confidential Information pursuant to any Third Party Request through all available legal processes, including, but not limited to, seeking to obtain any necessary protective orders.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Defend Against Third Party Requests. Each Party and Reviewing Representative may, at their sole discretion, defend against disclosure of Confidential Information pursuant to any Third Party Request. The decision to defend, and the extent of such defense, is entirely at the discretion of the Party or Reviewing Representative.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a contradiction by making the defense against disclosure of confidential information discretionary, conflicting with established legal principles regarding the protection of confidential information subject to legal process (e.g., subpoenas).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 45(d)(3)(A)
perturbation.law_citation: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 45(d)(3)(A)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification creates a scenario where parties can choose to disregard their duty to protect confidential information from improper disclosure in response to a valid subpoena or other legal demand, potentially conflicting with legal standards and the need to adhere to court orders, as outlined in the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, specifically Rule 45(d)(3)(A) which addresses protecting a person subject to a subpoena.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 6.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates defense against disclosure of confidential information in response to third-party requests. The changed text, however, makes this defense discretionary. The Law Explanation suggests this conflicts with the need to adhere to court orders, referencing Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45(d)(3)(A). While the scraped snippets provided focus on the form and contents of subpoenas (Snippet 1) and the purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (Snippet 2), the change from 'shall' to 'may' fundamentally alters the obligation. The original text necessitates action to protect confidential information, whereas the modified text permits inaction, directly contradicting the implicit legal expectation to protect privileged or confidential information when legally challenged.
